hook: You already know how to calculate the chance of flipping heads or rolling a six — but what happens when the situations get far more complex, like predicting how many students in your school might score above a certain grade, or figuring out the best strategy in a game show? Basic probability only takes you so far. In this unit, you'll build a powerful toolkit that lets you model real-world randomness with precision and confidence.
concept: Every topic in this unit is about quantifying uncertainty — turning 'maybe' into meaningful numbers. Together, they form the foundation of probability theory and statistical reasoning, unified by the idea of a probability distribution.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Sample Spaces and Basic Counting Principles
topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to systematically list and count all possible outcomes of an event using tools like the Fundamental Counting Principle, tree diagrams, and organized lists.
Master the difference between ordered arrangements (permutations) and unordered selections (combinations) so you can count large sets of outcomes efficiently using formulas like $\binom{n}{r}$.
Apply the binomial probability formula to calculate the likelihood of getting exactly $k$ successes in $n$ independent trials, such as the probability of making 7 out of 10 free throws.
You will learn to recognize and describe the famous bell-shaped curve, understanding how the mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$ control its center and spread.
Apply the Empirical Rule and $z$-scores to find the percentage of data that falls within any range, answering real questions like what fraction of test scores fall above 85.
You will learn to calculate the long-run average outcome of a random process using expected value $E(X) = \sum x \cdot P(x)$, and use it to evaluate fairness in games, insurance, and decision-making.
